title: Uplink Failure Impact Guide
summary: Understand affected sites, users, critical applications, failover availability, and outage duration before an uplink failure becomes an operational surprise.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates outage severity and recovery pressure from the number of affected sites, users, application criticality, failover condition, and duration.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch network pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when reviewing WAN redundancy, branch resiliency, internet failover, maintenance exposure, service-provider dependency, or restoration priority.
bullets: Early network design planning.
Expansion, migration, or upgrade review.
Existing-system troubleshooting or validation.
Explaining network tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical network planning terms.

items.label: Sites / Branches Affected
items.description: The number of locations affected by the uplink failure.
items.whyItMatters: More affected locations increase coordination, user impact, and restoration priority.
items.commonMistake: Treating one failed uplink as isolated when it carries multiple sites.

items.label: Users Affected
items.description: The number of users depending on the failed or degraded uplink.
items.whyItMatters: User count helps translate technical failure into operational impact.
items.commonMistake: Counting only IT devices and not the people behind the service.

items.label: Critical Apps
items.description: The criticality of applications depending on the uplink.
items.whyItMatters: Critical applications increase severity even when user count is modest.
items.commonMistake: Judging impact by bandwidth alone and ignoring what the link carries.

links.label: Check bandwidth pressure for critical traffic
links.note: Use this when critical apps share bandwidth with other traffic.

items.label: Failover Available?
items.description: Whether a secondary ISP, LTE path, alternate route, or limited backup path is available.
items.whyItMatters: Failover determines whether the outage is a degraded-service event or a full interruption.
items.commonMistake: Assuming failover exists without checking its usable capacity.

links.label: Compare primary and backup capacity
links.note: Use this when the backup path may be smaller than normal demand.

items.label: Estimated Outage Duration (minutes)
items.description: The expected length of the failure or degraded state.
items.whyItMatters: Longer outages increase severity, communication burden, and operational risk.
items.commonMistake: Only considering whether a failure happens, not how long users remain affected.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means failover and impact appear manageable. Watch means the outage scenario deserves review. Risk means the failure may create significant user, application, or business impact.
bullets: Healthy: the entered assumptions leave practical operating margin.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumption deserves review before relying on it.
Risk: reduce demand, increase capacity, change the topology, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most network planning mistakes come from checking one layer or one average value while ignoring peaks, overhead, path behavior, failure conditions, and growth.
bullets: Planning from theoretical line rate instead of usable sustained throughput.
Sizing for calm-period average traffic while ignoring peaks, bursts, retransmits, and maintenance activity.
Treating one network layer in isolation even though access, aggregation, WAN, VPN, and endpoint behavior interact.
Forgetting to document assumptions before the design becomes hardware, cabling, or service-provider work.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, firewall/router/switch limits, service-provider terms, cabling validation, monitoring data, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Real measurements from the installed network are better than estimates whenever they are available.
Protocol overhead, encapsulation, device behavior, cabling, switching, routing, and WAN conditions can change the result.
Healthy results still need validation against the actual switch, firewall, router, ISP, tunnel, or endpoint platform being used.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, troubleshoot, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, vendor validation, production testing, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, firmware, service-provider limits, and network architecture being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations for performance, outage behavior, security, and operational recovery.
Document assumptions before implementation and revisit them as traffic or topology changes.
